Hands-on Exercise 3 - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R

Author

Yang Yang

3.1 Learning Outcome

In this hands-on exercise, we will learn how to create interactive data visualisations in R. The focus is on using ggiraph and plotly to move beyond static charts and allow readers to explore the data directly.

By the end of this exercise, we will be able to:

  • create interactive ggplot graphics with ggiraph,
  • add tooltip, hover, and click effects,
  • create interactive scatter plots with plotly,
  • convert ggplot charts into interactive charts,
  • build coordinated views across multiple charts and tables.

3.2 Getting Started

Before creating the interactive charts, the required R packages need to be loaded. These packages support interactive graphics, data manipulation, interactive tables, and linked visualisation.

  • ggiraph is used to make ggplot graphics interactive.
  • plotly is used to create interactive web-based statistical charts.
  • DT is used to display interactive data tables.
  • tidyverse is used for data import, wrangling, and ggplot2 graphics.
  • patchwork is used to combine several ggplot2 charts into one layout.
  • crosstalk is used for linked interactions between widgets.

3.3 Importing Data

The dataset used in this exercise is Exam_data.csv. It contains student-level examination scores and related student information. The data is imported with read_csv() and saved as a tibble called exam_data.

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")
TipSmall note

The file Exam_data.csv must be placed in the same folder as Hands-on_Ex03.qmd. If the file is stored somewhere else, the path inside read_csv() must be changed.

3.4 Interactive Data Visualisation - ggiraph methods

ggiraph is an extension of ggplot2 that turns static ggplot charts into interactive SVG graphics. This is useful when we want readers to inspect individual data points instead of only looking at the overall pattern.

The main interactive features are:

  • tooltip: shows information when the mouse hovers over a data point.
  • onclick: runs a JavaScript action when an element is clicked.
  • data_id: links graphic elements to an identifier, which is useful for hover and coordinated highlighting.

3.4.1 Tooltip effect with tooltip aesthetic

This first example creates an interactive dot plot of Maths scores. Each dot represents a student, and the tooltip shows the student ID when the reader hovers over the dot.

  • View
  • Code
p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Interactive Dot Plot of Maths Scores",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)

The chart is created in two steps. First, geom_dotplot_interactive() builds the interactive ggplot object. Then, girafe() converts the ggplot object into an interactive visual that can be displayed on an HTML page.

3.5 Interactivity

The simplest form of interactivity is a tooltip. When the reader hovers over a dot, the student ID appears. This makes the chart more useful because individual observations can be inspected without adding too many permanent labels to the plot.

3.5.1 Displaying multiple information on tooltip

A tooltip can also show more than one piece of information. In this example, the tooltip combines student ID and class into one text field.

  • View
  • Code
exam_data <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    tooltip_basic = paste0(
      "Student ID: ", ID,
      "\nClass: ", CLASS
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_basic),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Maths Scores with Student and Class Tooltip",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)

Here, a new column called tooltip_basic is created. This column stores the text that will appear in the tooltip. This is cleaner than writing long tooltip logic directly inside aes().

3.6 Interactivity

This section expands the use of ggiraph by adding customised tooltip styles, statistical summaries, hover effects, click actions, and coordinated multiple views.

3.6.1 Customising Tooltip style

The tooltip does not have to use the default style. We can use CSS to control its background colour, text colour, font weight, padding, and shape.

  • View
  • Code
tooltip_css <- paste0(
  "background-color:", my_dark, ";",
  "color:white;",
  "padding:6px 9px;",
  "border-radius:6px;",
  "font-weight:600;",
  "font-size:12px;"
)

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Tooltip Styling with ggiraph",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)
TipWhat can we learn?

opts_tooltip() controls how the tooltip looks. This is useful when the chart needs to match a website or report colour style. Here, the tooltip uses a dark background with white text to match the muted teal visual design.

3.6.2 Displaying statistics on tooltip

Tooltips can also show calculated statistics. In this example, the chart summarises Maths scores by race and displays the mean with the standard error range in the tooltip.

  • View
  • Code
tooltip_stat <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = 0.01) {
  mean_value <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem_value <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean Maths score:", mean_value, "+/-", sem_value)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
                   aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(
      y = MATHS,
      tooltip = after_stat(tooltip_stat(y, ymax))
    ),
    fun.data = "mean_se",
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    linewidth = 0.35,
    color = my_dark
  ) +
  labs(title = "Mean Maths Scores by Race",
       subtitle = "Tooltip shows mean and standard error",
       x = "Race",
       y = "Mean Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = gg_point,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)

This example is more advanced because the tooltip is not taken directly from the raw dataset. Instead, it is built from summary statistics calculated by stat_summary().

3.6.3 Hover effect with data_id aesthetic

The data_id aesthetic is used to connect visual elements to an identifier. When the mouse moves over one element, related elements with the same data_id can be highlighted.

  • View
  • Code
p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = CLASS),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_soft_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Hover Highlight by Class",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = hover_css),
    opts_hover_inv(css = hover_inv_css)
  )
)

When the reader hovers over one dot, dots from the same class are highlighted. This makes class-level grouping easier to notice.

3.6.4 Styling hover effect

The hover effect can be styled with CSS. Here, the selected dots become stronger, while the non-selected dots become faded.

  • View
  • Code
p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = CLASS),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_soft_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Custom Hover Style",
       subtitle = "Highlighted points stay strong while other points fade",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = hover_css),
    opts_hover_inv(css = hover_inv_css)
  )
)
TipWhat can we learn?

opts_hover() controls the selected item, while opts_hover_inv() controls the non-selected items. This is useful because interactivity should guide attention, not just decorate the chart.

3.6.5 Combining tooltip and hover effect

Tooltip and hover effects can be used together. The tooltip gives detailed information, while the hover effect highlights related points.

  • View
  • Code
p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(
      tooltip = CLASS,
      data_id = CLASS
    ),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_soft_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Tooltip and Hover Effect Combined",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css),
    opts_hover(css = hover_css),
    opts_hover_inv(css = hover_inv_css)
  )
)

In this chart, the tooltip shows the class, and the hover effect highlights students from the same class. This gives both detail and pattern recognition in the same visual.

3.6.6 Click effect with onclick

The onclick aesthetic allows an action to happen when the reader clicks on a chart element. In web-based visualisation, this can be used to open a link or trigger another action.

WarningWarning

Click actions must be stored as valid JavaScript strings. If the JavaScript syntax is wrong, the chart may still render, but the click action will not work correctly.

  • View
  • Code
exam_data <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    onclick = sprintf(
      "window.open('%s')",
      "https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school"
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(onclick = onclick,
        tooltip = paste0("Click to open school finder\nStudent ID: ", ID)),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Click Interaction with onclick",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 7,
  height_svg = 7 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)

This example shows how interactive charts can connect the visual object to an external web action. The chart is no longer just for viewing; it can also become a navigation tool.

3.6.7 Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph

Coordinated multiple views allow different charts to respond to the same interaction. In this example, Maths and English dot plots are placed side by side. Because both charts use the same student ID as data_id, hovering over a student in one chart highlights the same student in the other chart.

  • View
  • Code
p1 <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = ID,
        tooltip = paste0("Student ID: ", ID, "\nMaths: ", MATHS)),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Maths Scores",
       x = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p2 <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = ID,
        tooltip = paste0("Student ID: ", ID, "\nEnglish: ", ENGLISH)),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot",
    fill = my_soft_teal,
    color = my_dark,
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "English Scores",
       x = "English score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

girafe(
  code = print(p1 + p2),
  width_svg = 9,
  height_svg = 4,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css),
    opts_hover(css = hover_css),
    opts_hover_inv(css = hover_inv_css)
  )
)
TipWhat can we learn?

The key argument is data_id = ID. Because the same student ID is used in both plots, ggiraph can recognise that the two points refer to the same observation.

3.7 Interactive Data Visualisation - plotly methods

plotly creates interactive web graphics in R. It can be used directly with plot_ly(), or it can convert existing ggplot2 charts into interactive charts with ggplotly().

There are two common ways to use plotly:

  • plot_ly() creates an interactive chart directly.
  • ggplotly() converts a ggplot chart into an interactive plotly chart.

3.7.1 Creating an interactive scatter plot: plot_ly() method

This example creates a basic interactive scatter plot using plot_ly(). The x-axis shows Maths scores, while the y-axis shows English scores.

  • View
  • Code
plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~MATHS,
  y = ~ENGLISH,
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers",
  marker = list(
    color = my_teal,
    size = 8,
    opacity = 0.75,
    line = list(color = my_dark, width = 0.6)
  ),
  text = ~paste(
    "Student ID:", ID,
    "<br>Class:", CLASS,
    "<br>Maths:", MATHS,
    "<br>English:", ENGLISH
  ),
  hoverinfo = "text"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "English Scores versus Maths Scores",
    xaxis = list(title = "Maths score", range = c(0, 100)),
    yaxis = list(title = "English score", range = c(0, 100))
  )

This chart is interactive immediately. The reader can hover, zoom, pan, and inspect exact values without extra ggplot layers.

3.7.2 Working with visual variable: plot_ly() method

A visual variable can be mapped to a data field. Here, RACE is mapped to colour, allowing the reader to compare score patterns across groups.

  • View
  • Code
plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~ENGLISH,
  y = ~MATHS,
  color = ~RACE,
  colors = c(my_teal, my_soft_teal, "#D9B08C", "#5B6770", "#A0A0A0"),
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers",
  marker = list(
    size = 8,
    opacity = 0.75,
    line = list(color = my_dark, width = 0.4)
  ),
  text = ~paste(
    "Student ID:", ID,
    "<br>Race:", RACE,
    "<br>English:", ENGLISH,
    "<br>Maths:", MATHS
  ),
  hoverinfo = "text"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Maths and English Scores by Race",
    xaxis = list(title = "English score", range = c(0, 100)),
    yaxis = list(title = "Maths score", range = c(0, 100))
  )
TipInteraction to try

Click one race category in the legend. Plotly will hide or show that group, making it easier to compare one group at a time.

3.7.3 Creating an interactive scatter plot: ggplotly() method

If a chart has already been created with ggplot2, ggplotly() can convert it into an interactive chart. This is useful when we want to keep ggplot2 syntax but still add web interactivity.

  • View
  • Code
p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH,
                text = paste0(
                  "Student ID: ", ID,
                  "\nClass: ", CLASS,
                  "\nMaths: ", MATHS,
                  "\nEnglish: ", ENGLISH
                ))) +
  geom_point(color = my_teal,
             size = 2,
             alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              linewidth = 0.5,
              color = my_dark,
              se = TRUE) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Interactive ggplotly Scatter Plot",
       x = "Maths score",
       y = "English score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")

Only one extra function, ggplotly(), is needed to turn the ggplot object into an interactive plotly chart.

3.7.4 Coordinated Multiple Views with plotly

Plotly can also create coordinated views. The idea is to build two related charts and link them through shared data. When a point is selected in one chart, the related point can be highlighted in another chart.

  • View
  • Code
d <- highlight_key(exam_data, ~ID)

p1 <- ggplot(data = d,
             aes(x = MATHS,
                 y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(color = my_teal,
             size = 1.8,
             alpha = 0.75) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "English versus Maths",
       x = "Maths score",
       y = "English score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p2 <- ggplot(data = d,
             aes(x = MATHS,
                 y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(color = my_soft_teal,
             size = 1.8,
             alpha = 0.75) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Science versus Maths",
       x = "Maths score",
       y = "Science score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

subplot(
  ggplotly(p1),
  ggplotly(p2),
  nrows = 1,
  titleX = TRUE,
  titleY = TRUE
) %>%
  highlight(
    on = "plotly_selected",
    off = "plotly_deselect",
    persistent = TRUE
  )
TipWhat can we learn?

highlight_key() creates shared data. This shared data allows the two plotly charts to recognise that selected points refer to the same student.

3.8 Interactive Data Visualisation - crosstalk methods

crosstalk allows different HTML widgets to communicate with one another. This is useful when we want a chart and a table to respond to the same selection.

3.8.1 Interactive Data Table: DT package

The DT package displays an R data frame as an interactive table. Readers can search, sort, and browse the data directly on the web page.

  • View
  • Code
DT::datatable(
  exam_data,
  class = "compact stripe hover",
  rownames = FALSE,
  options = list(
    pageLength = 8,
    scrollX = TRUE
  )
)

The table gives readers a direct way to inspect the dataset. This is useful because interactive charts show patterns, while tables allow checking exact records.

3.8.2 Linked brushing: crosstalk method

Linked brushing connects a chart and a table. Selecting data points in the chart can highlight or filter the corresponding records in the table.

  • View
  • Code
d <- highlight_key(exam_data, ~ID)

p <- ggplot(d,
            aes(ENGLISH,
                MATHS)) +
  geom_point(color = my_teal,
             size = 1.8,
             alpha = 0.75) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Linked Brushing between Chart and Table",
       x = "English score",
       y = "Maths score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

gg <- highlight(
  ggplotly(p),
  on = "plotly_selected",
  off = "plotly_deselect",
  persistent = TRUE
)

crosstalk::bscols(
  widths = c(5, 7),
  gg,
  DT::datatable(
    d,
    class = "compact stripe hover",
    rownames = FALSE,
    options = list(
      pageLength = 8,
      scrollX = TRUE
    )
  )
)
WarningWarning

bscols() may load Bootstrap styling into the page. This is normal, but it can slightly affect the visual appearance of surrounding page elements.

TipWhat can we learn?

The chart and table are linked through the same shared data object. This makes it possible to move from visual exploration to exact record checking without leaving the page.

3.9 Reference

3.9.1 ggiraph

ggiraph is useful when a ggplot chart needs lightweight interaction, especially tooltip, hover, and click effects. It works well when the goal is to keep the grammar of ggplot2 but make the output more exploratory.

Useful topics to review:

  • interactive geoms such as geom_point_interactive() and geom_dotplot_interactive(),
  • tooltip styling with opts_tooltip(),
  • hover styling with opts_hover() and opts_hover_inv(),
  • linking observations with data_id.

3.9.2 plotly for R

plotly is useful for creating interactive statistical graphics directly, or for converting ggplot2 charts into interactive web charts.

Useful topics to review:

  • plot_ly() for direct plotly charts,
  • ggplotly() for converting ggplot charts,
  • subplot() for placing multiple plotly charts together,
  • highlight_key() and highlight() for coordinated interactions.
TipFinal takeaway

ggiraph is closer to ggplot2 and is useful for controlled tooltip and hover design. plotly is stronger for zooming, selecting, and building interactive web charts quickly. DT and crosstalk are useful when the reader needs to connect charts with exact data records.
